passages.explanation: Jesus enters Jericho and is passing through. A man named Zacchaeus is there — he's a chief tax collector and is wealthy. He's trying to see who Jesus is but can't because he's short, and the crowd is in the way. So he runs ahead and climbs a sycamore-fig tree. When Jesus reaches the spot, he looks up: Zacchaeus, come down immediately — I must stay at your house today. Zacchaeus hurries down and welcomes him gladly. The crowd mutters: he's gone to be the guest of a sinner. But Zacchaeus stands up and says: Lord, here and now I give half of my possessions to the poor, and if I've cheated anybody out of anything, I'll pay back four times the amount. Jesus says: today salvation has come to this house, because this man too is a son of Abraham. For the Son of Man came to seek and save the lost. Nobody asks Zacchaeus to give away anything — his generosity is spontaneous, the evidence that something has changed inside. Jesus' final statement is the mission statement for Luke's Gospel.

Because they're near Jerusalem and the people think the kingdom of God is going to appear at once, Jesus tells a parable. A man of noble birth goes to a distant country to be appointed king and then return. He calls ten servants and gives them each a mina — about three months' wages — and says: put this money to work until I come back. But his subjects hate him and send a delegation: we don't want this man to be our king. When he returns as king, he calls the servants. The first has earned ten more minas — he's put in charge of ten cities. The second has earned five — put in charge of five cities. But one servant has hidden the mina in a cloth. He says: I was afraid of you because you're a hard man. The king rebukes him: if you knew I was a hard man, why didn't you at least put the money on deposit? He takes the mina and gives it to the one who has ten. As for the enemies who didn't want him to be king — bring them here and kill them in front of me. The parable corrects the expectation that Jesus will establish his kingdom immediately. There will be a departure, a delay, and a return. In the meantime, his servants are expected to work with what they've been given. Faithfulness during the delay is the test.

Jesus goes on ahead, going up to Jerusalem. As he approaches Bethphage and Bethany at the Mount of Olives, he sends two disciples to the village ahead: you'll find a colt tied there that no one has ever ridden. Untie it and bring it here. If anyone asks why, say: the Lord needs it. They find it just as he said. The owners ask why they're untying it. They answer: the Lord needs it. They bring the colt to Jesus, throw their cloaks on it, and put Jesus on it. As he rides along, people spread their cloaks on the road. When he reaches the descent of the Mount of Olives, the whole crowd of disciples begins joyfully praising God in loud voices for all the miracles they've seen: blessed is the king who comes in the name of the Lord! Peace in heaven and glory in the highest! Some Pharisees in the crowd say: teacher, rebuke your disciples! Jesus answers: I tell you, if they keep quiet, the stones will cry out. Jesus enters Jerusalem in a way that deliberately fulfils Zechariah 9:9 — a king riding on a donkey, humble and on a colt. The Pharisees recognise the messianic claim and want it silenced. Jesus says the declaration is unstoppable.

As Jesus approaches Jerusalem and sees the city, he weeps over it: if you, even you, had only known on this day what would bring you peace — but now it is hidden from your eyes. The days will come when your enemies will build an embankment against you and encircle you and hem you in on every side. They will dash you to the ground, you and the children within your walls. They will not leave one stone on another, because you didn't recognise the time of God's coming to you. Jesus prophesies the destruction of Jerusalem, which Rome will carry out in AD 70. The triumphal entry and the weeping happen in the same moment — the crowd is celebrating while Jesus is grieving over what he knows is coming.

Jesus enters the temple courts and begins driving out those who are selling. He tells them: it is written — my house will be a house of prayer. But you have made it a den of robbers. Every day he teaches at the temple. The chief priests, the teachers of the law, and the leaders among the people are trying to kill him. But they can't find any way to do it, because all the people hang on his words. The temple clearing is an acted parable — a prophet's symbolic action declaring that the temple has failed its purpose. The authorities want him dead but the crowd's devotion protects him. For now.
